content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
· 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Thomas J. Ballanco, Esq. 
Hannonic Engineering, Inc. 
P.O. Box 492 
Weaverville, CA 96093 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-16-080 
Dear Mr. Ballanco: 
June 22, 2016 
This letter responds to your request on behalf of Trinity County Planning Commissioner 
John Brower for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the 
"Act").1 Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section 1090. 
QUESTION 
Under the Act, may Commissioner Brower participate as a Planning Commissioner in 
discussions and recommendations for the commercial cannabis land use ordinance, where 
Commissioner Brower is a member and paid employee of Alive Botanicals, LLC, a research, 
development, marketing business and distributor of cannabis-based therapeutic products. 
CONCLUSION 
Yes. The decision in question will not have a reasonably foreseeable, material financial 
effect on the Planning Commissioner's financial interests. 
FACTS 
Commissioner Brower was appointed to the Trinity Planning Commission to represent 
District 4 on March 23, 2016. He resides in District 4, in a Rural Residential zoned property, and 
his homeowners' association agreement prohibits cannabis cultivation. There are currently at least 
3,000 existing cannabis cultivation sites in Trinity County that account for more than 30% of the 
privately owned parcels in the county. While there are cultivation sites throughout the county, 
Commissioner Brower's District 4, and District 5 have the largest number of per capita cultivation 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 8 I 000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A -16-080 
Page No. 2 
sites (more than 40% of the privately owned parcels in those districts). Commissioner Brower is 
currently a member and paid employee of Alive Botanicals, LLC, located in Redway, Humboldt 
County, California. 
Alive Botanicals, LLC ("AB") is in the business of researching, developing, marketing and 
distributing therapeutic products made from cannabidiol ("CBD"), a nonpsychoactive component of 
medicinal cannabis. These therapeutic CBD products are provided to medicinal cannabis patient 
members, affiliated collectives, and dispensaries throughout California in accordance with existing 
state law. Mr. Brower is paid by AB for his research and development work, for consultation 
services he provides to AB, and to its affiliate collectives; and for his education and outreach 
activities. Mr. Brower's ownership share and annual compensation arc both in excess of $2,000. 
AB's source for its plant materials is the medicinal cannabis collective organized under 
California state law called CBD Now! Collective ("Collective"). The Collective and its members 
who provide plant material are located in Humboldt County. Under the "collective model" excess 
plant material has been sold to AB at actual production cost. 
The Collective has particular growing practices that set its plant material apart. All plant 
material is grown outdoors, without the use of chemicals or animal poisons.2 It also tests all plant 
material for cannabinoid content. Three other collectives with a similar product are located in 
Trinity County. Af3 currently has no plans to use another supply source anw states that the sourcing 
of plant material is not an issue for the company. 
The recently enacted Medical Marijuana Regulation and Safety Act 3 ("MMRSA") does 
away with the collective model and no longer prohibits businesses from cultivating or distributing 
medical cannabis for profit. Under the MMRSA, businesses will operate under direct commercial 
licensing for medicinal cannabis cultivation. It is not known what impact MMRSA will have on 
suppliers of CBD-rich materials, as much will depend on emerging market forces. 
Under the MMRSA, all commercial cultivation of medical cannabis will be subject to local 
land use regulations and permits. The Board of Supervisors has instructed the Planning Commission 
that Trinity County will not ban commercial medicinal cannabis cultivation and cultivation will be 
permitted on at least some parcels in Trinity County, in line with the MMRSA. The Planning 
1 Pesticide use in the production of medical cannabis is a current concern for the industry. The Legislative 
findings for Assembly Bill 243 of 2015, c. 688, state that the use of pesticides on cannabis crops intended for human 
consumption is not currently regulated by the federal government, cannabis crops often can contain pesticide residues, 
and lawful growers are urging the state to provide guidance on whether pesticides currently used at most sites are safe 
for human consumption. (See Bus. & Prof. Code Section 19331.) 
3 Medical Marijuana Regulation and Safety Act ("MM RSA") consists of three separate bills that were enacted 
together on September 11, 2015 and effective January I, 2016. The Act creates a comprehensive state licensing system 
for the commercial cultivation, manufacture, retail sale, transport, distribution, delivery, and testing of medical 
cannabis. All licenses must also be approved by local governments. It is anticipated that the state will need until January 
2018 to set up the necessary agencies, infonnation systems, and regulations to begin issuing licenses. In the interim, 
local governments may choose to adopt new ordinances to pennit or license local businesses in preparation for state 
licensing. Facilities currently operating in accordance with state and local laws may continue to do so until such time a,; 
their license applications are approved or denied. 


File No. A -16-080 
Page No. 3 
Commission is tasked with making recommendations regarding appropriate zoning districts, parcel 
sizes, setbacks, fencing requirements and other factors related to land use. 
According a staff report, the Planning Commission held a workshop on March I 0, 2016, and 
reached consensus on the following items that will be scheduled for a vote to confirm, modify or 
reverse consensus: 
1) Indoor vs. Outdoor Grow 
2) Appropriate Grow Limits and Boundary Definition 
3) Parcel Size and Linkage with Commercial Grow Area Size 
4) Scope of Commercial Activities Allowed (e.g., cultivation, processing, 
manufacturing and distribution4 per the MMRSA) 
5) Zone District/General Plan Designation Areas in Which Commercial MM is 
Permissible 
6) Appropriate Setback Requirements for MM Uses 
7) Appropriate Fencing Standards 
8) Requiring a permitted residence on all properties where MM is being 
cultivated. 
(Trinity County Planning Department Staff Report, March 24, 2016.) 
ANALYSIS 
Section 87100 prohibits any state or local public official from making, participating in 
making, or using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the 
official has a financial interest. As a member of the Trinity County Planning Commission, 
Commissioner Brower is a public official. Here, the government decision is making 
recommendations regarding appropriate zoning districts, parcel sizes, setbacks, fencing 
requirements and other factors under the new commercial medicinal cannabis cultivation land use 
ordinance. 
Section 87103 defines financial interests to include: 
• 
Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment 
worth at least $2,000 and any business entity in which the public official is a 
director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management. 
(Section 87103(a) and (d).) 
• 
Real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more. 
(Section 87103(b).) 
• 
Any source of income, such as a business entity, from which the official has 
received income of $500 or more within 12 months before the decision. This also 
F According to Com. Brower, Trinity County currently bans medical cannabis dispensaries. (Ord. No. 315-
795). Thus, "distnbution" could involve distribution to dispensaries located in other counties. The dispensary ban is not 
currently at issue and any change would require a separate action by the Board of Supervisors. 


File No. A -16-080 
Page No. 4 
includes income from any client of the business entity of at least $500, provided to 
and received by the public official within 12 months before the decision is made. 
(Section 87103(c).) 
• 
An interest in the official's personal finances, including those of the official's immediate 
family. This is known as the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103.) 
Our focus is on Commissioner Brower's ownership interest in Alive Botanicals, LLC and in 
his position as a paid employee, as financial interests under the Act. There are no facts to suggest that 
the decision will have any financial effect on Commissioner Brower or his immediate family's personal 
finances or real property interest. 
To determine if Commissioner Brower will have a prohibited conflict of interest under the 
Act, we determine if it is reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will have a material 
financial effect on his ownership interest in AB, and on AB as a source of employment income. 
(Regulation 18700(d).) 
Foreseeability: 
Regulation 1870 I (b) sets out the appropriate standard to determine foreseeability where the 
financial interest is not explicitly involved in the decision. 5 Generally, a financial effect is 
reasonably foreseeable where it "can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than 
hypothetical or theoretical . . .  absent extraordinary circumstances beyond the public official's control." 
(Regulation 1870 I (b ). ) A financial effect need not be likely to be considered reasonably foreseeable. 
(Id.) 
Regulation 18701 (b) provides some general guidelines to consider to determine 
foreseeability: 
(I) The extent to which the occurrence of the financial efect is contingent upon 
intervening events, not including future governmental decisions by the official's 
agency, or any other agency appointed by or subject to the budgetary control of the 
official's agency. 
(2) Whether the public official should anticipate a financial efect on his or her 
financial interest as a potential outcome under normal circumstances when using 
appropriate due diligence and care. 
(3) Whether the public official has a financial interest that is of the type that would 
typically be affected by the terms of the governmental decision or whether the 
'Commissioner Brower's financial interests are not explicitly involved in the decision at issue. AB is not a 
named party, and is not the subject of the decision pursuant to Regulation 1870 l(a). This regulation states as follows: 
§ 18701. Detetmining Whether a Financial Effect Is Reasonably Foreseeable. (a) Financial Interest Explicitly Involved: 
A financial effect on a financial interest is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the financial interest is a named 
party in, or the subject of, a governmental decision before the official or the official's agency. A financial interest is the 
subject of a proceeding if the decision involves the issuance, renewal. approval, denial or revocation of any license, 
petmit. or other entitlement to, or contract with, the financial interest, and includes any governmental decision affecting 
a real property financial interest as described in Regulation l 8702.2(a)()) -(6). 


File No. A -16-080 
Page No. 5 
governmental decision is of the type that would be expected to have a financial effect 
on businesses and individuals similarly situated to those businesses and individuals in 
which the public official has a financial interest. 
( 4) Whether a reasonable inference can be made that the financial effects of the 
governmental decision on the public official's financial interest might compromise a 
public official's ability to act in a manner consistent with his or her duty to act in the 
best interests of the public. 
(5) Whether the governmental decision will provide or deny an opportunity, or create 
an advantage or disadvantage for one of the official's financial interests, including 
whether the financial interest may be entitled to compete or be eligible for a benefit 
resulting from the decision. 
(6) Whether the public official has the type of financial interest that would cause a 
similarly situated person to weigh the advantages and disadvantages of the 
governmental decision on his or her financial interest in formulating a position. 
Materiality: Business Entity and Business Entity as Source of Income 
The standard to determine materiality, for a business entity and business entity as source of 
income, including salary, 6 is set forth in Regulation 18702. l (b) (business entity, not explicitly 
involved). Materiality is found if "a prudent person with sufficient infonnation would find it is 
reasonably foreseeable that the decision's financial effect would contribute to a change ... in the 
value of a privately-held business entity." (Regulation 18702.1 (b).) The regulation notes "decisions 
that may be applicable" to include, as relevant, decisions that: 
( 1) Authorize, prohibit, regulate or otherwise establish conditions for an activity in 
which the business entity is engaged; 
(2) Increase or decrease the amount of competition in the field in which the 
business entity is engaged; 
(Regulation 18702. 1 (b).) 
The Trinity Board of Supervisors has instructed the Planning Commission that the county 
will permit commercial medicinal cannabis cultivation on at least some parcels in Trinity County 
under a new land use ordinance. The Planning Commission is in the process of preparing 
recommendations relating to appropriate zoning districts, parcel sizes, setbacks, fencing 
requirements and other factors related to land use. The Planning Commission decisions will involve 
the authorization and regulation of where, and under what conditions, commercial medicinal 
cannabis cultivation can occur. 
6 See Regulation 18702.3(a)(4). Where the source of income, including salary, is a business entity, Regulation 
18702.3 refers back to the standard of applied in 18702.1 for business entities. 


File No. A -16-080 
Page No. 6 
Here, it is not reasonably foreseeable that the Planning Commission's recommendations will 
have a material financial effect on Alive Botanicals, LLC or Commissioner Brower's income as an 
employee. AB is not engaged in the business of growing cannabis. The land use recommendations 
will impact growers in Trinity County and are focused on cultivation issues. AB is in the business 
of researching, developing, marketing and distributing therapeutic products made from CBD. AB's 
supplier is located in Humboldt county, as are its growers. The MMRSA 's shift from collectives 
and allowance of cultivating for profit will change the industry, supply costs, and intervening 
factors could additionally change the plant material supply picture. However, AB currently has no 
plans or need to obtain its plant materials from Trinity County growers. There are no facts showing 
that the land use decisions at issue, which will impact Trinity growers, would materially alter AB's 
business costs for purchasing its specific plant material supplies. The .. distribution" discussion 
appears to be limited at this time to distribution by County growers to dispensaries in permitted 
counties.7 
Commissioner Brower does not have a reasonably foreseeable, material financial interest in 
the Planning Commission's decisions relating to the new commercial medicinal cannabis 
cultivation land use ordinance. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
LKH: jgl 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Counsel 
By: 
L. Karen Harrison 
Counsel, Legal Division 
1 In the e\'ent that decisions do involve research, development, marketing and distnbution of therapeutic 
products; i.e., the activities that AB is engaged, please contact the FPPC for additional advice on possible conflicts of 
interest,

development, marketing business and distributor of cannabis-based therapeutic products.

Yes. The decision in question will not have a reasonably foreseeable, material financial 
effect on the Planning Commissioner's financial interests.

cultivated. 
(Trinity County Planning Department Staff Report, March 24, 2016.)

(Section 87103(b).)

received income of $500 or more within 12 months before the decision. This also

(Section 87103(c).)

discussions and recommendations for the commercial cannabis land use ordinance, where 
Commissioner Brower is a member and paid employee of Alive Botanicals, LLC, a research, 
development, marketing business and distributor of cannabis-based therapeutic products.

CONCLUSION: Yes. The decision in question will not have a reasonably foreseeable, material financial 
effect on the Planning Commissioner's financial interests.
